scene_title: A room of state in the castle

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Though yet of Hamlet our dear brother’s death

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: The memory be green, and that it us befitted

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: To bear our hearts in grief and our whole kingdom

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: To be contracted in one brow of woe,

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Yet so far hath discretion fought with nature

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: That we with wisest sorrow think on him,

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Together with remembrance of ourselves.

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Therefore our sometime sister, now our queen,

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: The imperial jointress to this warlike state,

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Have we, as ’twere with a defeated joy—

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: With an auspicious and a dropping eye,

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: With mirth in funeral and with dirge in marriage,

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: In equal scale weighing delight and dole—

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Taken to wife: nor have we herein barr’d

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Your better wisdoms, which have freely gone

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: With this affair along. For all, our thanks.

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Now follows, that you know, young Fortinbras,

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Holding a weak supposal of our worth,

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Or thinking by our late dear brother’s death

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Our state to be disjoint and out of frame,

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Colleagued with the dream of his advantage,

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: He hath not fail’d to pester us with message,

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Importing the surrender of those lands

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Lost by his father, with all bonds of law,

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: To our most valiant brother. So much for him.

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Now for ourself and for this time of meeting:

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Thus much the business is: we have here writ

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: To Norway, uncle of young Fortinbras—

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Who, impotent and bed-rid, scarcely hears

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Of this his nephew’s purpose—to suppress

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: His further gait herein; in that the levies,

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: The lists and full proportions, are all made

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Out of his subject: and we here dispatch

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: You, good Cornelius, and you, Voltimand,

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: For bearers of this greeting to old Norway;

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Giving to you no further personal power

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: To business with the king, more than the scope

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Of these delated articles allow.

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Farewell, and let your haste commend your duty.

lines.speaker: CORNELIUS VOLTIMAND
lines.text: In that and all things will we show our duty.

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: We doubt it nothing: heartily farewell.

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: And now, Laertes, what’s the news with you?

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: You told us of some suit; what is’t, Laertes?

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: You cannot speak of reason to the Dane,

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: And lose your voice: what wouldst thou beg, Laertes,

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: That shall not be my offer, not thy asking?

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: The head is not more native to the heart,

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: The hand more instrumental to the mouth,

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Than is the throne of Denmark to thy father.

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: What wouldst thou have, Laertes?

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: My dread lord,

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: Your leave and favour to return to France;

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: From whence though willingly I came to Denmark,

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: To show my duty in your coronation,

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: Yet now, I must confess, that duty done,

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: My thoughts and wishes bend again toward France

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: And bow them to your gracious leave and pardon.

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Have you your father’s leave? What says Polonius?

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: He hath, my lord, wrung from me my slow leave

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: By laboursome petition, and at last

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: Upon his will I seal’d my hard consent:

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: I do beseech you, give him leave to go.

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Take thy fair hour, Laertes; time be thine,

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: And thy best graces spend it at thy will!

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: But now, my cousin Hamlet, and my son—

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: A little more than kin, and less than kind.

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: How is it that the clouds still hang on you?

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Not so, my lord; I am too much i’ the sun.

lines.speaker: QUEEN
lines.text: Good Hamlet, cast thy nighted colour off,

lines.speaker: QUEEN
lines.text: And let thine eye look like a friend on Denmark.

lines.speaker: QUEEN
lines.text: Do not for ever with thy vailed lids

lines.speaker: QUEEN
lines.text: Seek for thy noble father in the dust:

lines.speaker: QUEEN
lines.text: Thou know’st ’tis common; all that lives must die,

lines.speaker: QUEEN
lines.text: Passing through nature to eternity.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Ay, madam, it is common.

lines.speaker: QUEEN
lines.text: If it be,

lines.speaker: QUEEN
lines.text: Why seems it so particular with thee?

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Seems, madam! nay, it is; I know not “seems.”

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: ’Tis not alone my inky cloak, good mother,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Nor customary suits of solemn black,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Nor windy suspiration of forced breath,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: No, nor the fruitful river in the eye,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Nor the dejected ’havior of the visage,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Together with all forms, moods, shapes of grief,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: That can denote me truly: these indeed seem,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: For they are actions that a man might play:

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: But I have that within which passeth show;

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: These but the trappings and the suits of woe.

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: ’Tis sweet and commendable in your nature, Hamlet,

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: To give these mourning duties to your father:

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: But, you must know, your father lost a father;

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: That father lost, lost his, and the survivor bound

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: In filial obligation for some term

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: To do obsequious sorrow: but to persever

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: In obstinate condolement is a course

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Of impious stubbornness; ’tis unmanly grief;

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: It shows a will most incorrect to heaven,

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: A heart unfortified, a mind impatient,

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: An understanding simple and unschool’d:

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: For what we know must be and is as common

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: As any the most vulgar thing to sense,

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Why should we in our peevish opposition

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Take it to heart? Fie! ’tis a fault to heaven,

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: A fault against the dead, a fault to nature,

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: To reason most absurd; whose common theme

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Is death of fathers, and who still hath cried,

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: From the first corse till he that died to-day,

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: “This must be so.” We pray you, throw to earth

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: This unprevailing woe, and think of us

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: As of a father: for let the world take note,

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: You are the most immediate to our throne;

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: And with no less nobility of love

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Than that which dearest father bears his son,

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Do I impart toward you. For your intent

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: In going back to school in Wittenberg,

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: It is most retrograde to our desire:

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: And we beseech you, bend you to remain

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Here, in the cheer and comfort of our eye,

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Our chiefest courtier, cousin, and our son.

lines.speaker: QUEEN
lines.text: Let not thy mother lose her prayers, Hamlet:

lines.speaker: QUEEN
lines.text: I pray thee, stay with us; go not to Wittenberg.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: I shall in all my best obey you, madam.

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Why, ’tis a loving and a fair reply:

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Be as ourself in Denmark. Madam, come;

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: This gentle and unforced accord of Hamlet

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Sits smiling to my heart: in grace whereof,

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: No jocund health that Denmark drinks to-day,

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: But the great cannon to the clouds shall tell,

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: And the king’s rouse the heavens shall bruit again,

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Re-speaking earthly thunder. Come away.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: O, that this too too solid flesh would melt,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Thaw and resolve itself into a dew!

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Or that the Everlasting had not fix’d

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: His canon ’gainst self-slaughter! O God! God!

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: How weary, stale, flat and unprofitable,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Seem to me all the uses of this world!

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Fie on’t! ah fie! ’tis an unweeded garden,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: That grows to seed; things rank and gross in nature

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Possess it merely. That it should come to this!

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: But two months dead: nay, not so much, not two:

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: So excellent a king; that was, to this,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Hyperion to a satyr; so loving to my mother

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: That he might not beteem the winds of heaven

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Visit her face too roughly. Heaven and earth!

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Must I remember? why, she would hang on him,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: As if increase of appetite had grown

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: By what it fed on: and yet, within a month—

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Let me not think on’t—Frailty, thy name is woman!—

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: A little month, or ere those shoes were old

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: With which she follow’d my poor father’s body,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Like Niobe, all tears:—why she, even she—

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: O, God! a beast, that wants discourse of reason,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Would have mourn’d longer—married with my uncle,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: My father’s brother, but no more like my father

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Than I to Hercules: within a month:

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Ere yet the salt of most unrighteous tears

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Had left the flushing in her galled eyes,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: She married. O, most wicked speed, to post

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: With such dexterity to incestuous sheets!

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: It is not nor it cannot come to good:

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: But break, my heart; for I must hold my tongue.

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Hail to your lordship!

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: I am glad to see you well:

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Horatio—or I do forget myself.

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: The same, my lord, and your poor servant ever.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Sir, my good friend; I’ll change that name with you:

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: And what make you from Wittenberg, Horatio?

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Marcellus?

lines.speaker: MARCELLUS
lines.text: My good lord—

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: I am very glad to see you. Good even, sir.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: But what, in faith, make you from Wittenberg?

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: A truant disposition, good my lord.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: I would not hear your enemy say so,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Nor shall you do mine ear that violence,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: To make it truster of your own report

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Against yourself: I know you are no truant.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: But what is your affair in Elsinore?

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: We’ll teach you to drink deep ere you depart.

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: My lord, I came to see your father’s funeral.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: I pray thee, do not mock me, fellow-student;

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: I think it was to see my mother’s wedding.

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Indeed, my lord, it follow’d hard upon.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Thrift, thrift, Horatio! the funeral baked meats

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Did coldly furnish forth the marriage tables.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Would I had met my dearest foe in heaven

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Or ever I had seen that day, Horatio!

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: My father!—methinks I see my father.

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Where, my lord?

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: In my mind’s eye, Horatio.

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: I saw him once; he was a goodly king.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: He was a man, take him for all in all,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: I shall not look upon his like again.

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: My lord, I think I saw him yesternight.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Saw? who?

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: My lord, the king your father.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: The king my father!

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Season your admiration for a while

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: With an attent ear, till I may deliver,

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Upon the witness of these gentlemen,

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: This marvel to you.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: For God’s love, let me hear.

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Two nights together had these gentlemen,

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Marcellus and Bernardo, on their watch,

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: In the dead vast and middle of the night,

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Been thus encounter’d. A figure like your father,

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Armed at point exactly, cap-a-pe,

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Appears before them, and with solemn march

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Goes slow and stately by them: thrice he walk’d

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: By their oppress’d and fear-surprised eyes,

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Within his truncheon’s length; whilst they, distill’d

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Almost to jelly with the act of fear,

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Stand dumb and speak not to him. This to me

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: In dreadful secrecy impart they did;

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: And I with them the third night kept the watch:

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Where, as they had deliver’d, both in time,

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Form of the thing, each word made true and good,

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: The apparition comes: I knew your father;

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: These hands are not more like.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: But where was this?

lines.speaker: MARCELLUS
lines.text: My lord, upon the platform where we watch’d.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Did you not speak to it?

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: My lord, I did;

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: But answer made it none: yet once methought

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: It lifted up its head and did address

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Itself to motion, like as it would speak;

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: But even then the morning cock crew loud,

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: And at the sound it shrunk in haste away,

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: And vanish’d from our sight.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: ’Tis very strange.

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: As I do live, my honour’d lord, ’tis true;

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: And we did think it writ down in our duty

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: To let you know of it.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Indeed, indeed, sirs, but this troubles me.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Hold you the watch to-night?

lines.speaker: MARCELLUS BERNARDO
lines.text: We do, my lord.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Arm’d, say you?

lines.speaker: MARCELLUS BERNARDO
lines.text: Arm’d, my lord.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: From top to toe?

lines.speaker: MARCELLUS BERNARDO
lines.text: My lord, from head to foot.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Then saw you not his face?

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: O, yes, my lord; he wore his beaver up.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: What, look’d he frowningly?

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: A countenance more in sorrow than in anger.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Pale or red?

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Nay, very pale.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: And fix’d his eyes upon you?

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Most constantly.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: I would I had been there.

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: It would have much amazed you.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Very like, very like. Stay’d it long?

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: While one with moderate haste might tell a hundred.

lines.speaker: MARCELLUS BERNARDO
lines.text: Longer, longer.

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Not when I saw’t.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: His beard was grizzled—no?

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: It was, as I have seen it in his life,

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: A sable silver’d.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: I will watch to-night;

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Perchance ’twill walk again.

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: I warrant it will.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: If it assume my noble father’s person,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: I’ll speak to it, though hell itself should gape

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: And bid me hold my peace. I pray you all,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: If you have hitherto conceal’d this sight,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Let it be tenable in your silence still;

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: And whatsoever else shall hap to-night,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Give it an understanding, but no tongue:

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: I will requite your loves. So, fare you well:

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Upon the platform, ’twixt eleven and twelve,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: I’ll visit you.

lines.speaker: ALL
lines.text: Our duty to your honour.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Your loves, as mine to you: farewell.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: My father’s spirit in arms! all is not well;

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: I doubt some foul play: would the night were come!

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Till then sit still, my soul: foul deeds will rise,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Though all the earth o’erwhelm them, to men’s eyes.

stage_directions.text: Enter King, Queen, Hamlet, Polonius, Laertes, Voltimand, Cornelius, Lords, and Attendants.

glosses.phrase: best graces spend it at thy will

too much in the sunshine (and punning on 'son')

glosses.phrase: trappings and the suits of woe
glosses.definition: outward show and clothing of grief
common thing to the senses (everyone dies)

coarse and corrupt by their nature

glosses.definition: before those shoes had become worn
mythological mother who wept endlessly over her dead children

glosses.phrase: dead vast and middle of the night

annotations.title: The rhetoric of the usurper
annotations.body: Claudius's opening speech is a masterclass in calculated balance: 'mirth in funeral,' 'dirge in marriage,' 'defeated joy,' 'auspicious and a dropping eye.' The rhetorical figure is oxymoron, and its density signals a speaker who needs language to do what his position cannot — reconcile mourning with celebration, legitimacy with haste. Elizabethan audiences would have heard a voice that sounds a great deal like their own Queen's accession speeches. What they would also have heard, and what Hamlet will later name, is that rhetorical poise functions here as concealment. The play's opening human voice is the voice of successful dissembling.

annotations.title: 'A little more than kin, and less than kind'
annotations.body: Hamlet's first line in the play is a pun that turns three times. 'Kin' means 'related,' now intensified by the incestuous marriage. 'Kind' means both 'natural' (he is too close in blood) and 'affectionate' (he feels nothing like it for Claudius). Beneath both runs the Old English sense of 'kind' as species or race: Claudius is not of Hamlet's kind. The line establishes a verbal strategy Hamlet will use for five acts: turning the simplest words against those who use them. When Claudius calls him 'son,' Hamlet hears 'sun'; when the King asks about his melancholy, Hamlet punctures 'seems' with 'I know not seems.' Wordplay is weaponry here, because open speech would be suicidal.

annotations.title: The first soliloquy: the problem of the remarriage
annotations.body: Hamlet's first soliloquy grieves less for his father than for his mother. 'Frailty, thy name is woman' is not a general philosophical claim but a specific accusation: Gertrude's 'most wicked speed' has poisoned the mourning. The speech moves through classical analogies — Hyperion to a satyr, Niobe all tears — and arrives at the unspeakable: 'incestuous sheets.' Elizabethan canon law did forbid marriage to a deceased brother's wife; Henry VIII's divorce from Catherine of Aragon had turned precisely on this point, and Shakespeare's audience would recognize the charge. What drives the soliloquy is not yet the ghost's murder but the mother's remarriage — a private sexual grief that the public political scene has forced Hamlet to swallow.

annotations.title: Hearsay and the delayed encounter
annotations.body: Shakespeare delays Hamlet's meeting with the ghost by staging Horatio's report of it first. The effect is to make the ghost simultaneously present (Horatio's testimony is detailed, specific) and absent (Hamlet must wait until 1.4 to see for himself). The narrative technique multiplies the voices that attest to the apparition, building evidentiary pressure; it also gives Hamlet time to react to the news before the ghost makes its revelation. 'Foul deeds will rise, / Though all the earth o'erwhelm them, to men's eyes' — this is Hamlet's first articulation of a principle the play will test to destruction: that hidden truths cannot stay hidden. The plot from here will press that claim against the opposing pressure of concealment.

trials.prompt: What rhetorical figure dominates Claudius's opening speech ('mirth in funeral,' 'dirge in marriage')?
options: Anaphora: repeated phrases at the start of successive clauses.
Oxymoron: the pairing of contradictory terms.
Hyperbole: deliberate exaggeration for emphasis.
Chiasmus: a reversed repetition of words.

trials.prompt: In Hamlet's first line — 'A little more than kin, and less than kind' — what does the pun on 'kind' convey?
options: Claudius is both overly familiar and insufficiently affectionate toward him.
Claudius is related by marriage but not by blood.
He means the word only in its modern sense of 'generous.'
He is contrasting Claudius to his father's courteous manner.

trials.prompt: What does Claudius deny Hamlet at the end of their first exchange?
options: Permission to return to his university at Wittenberg.
The right to bear arms at court.
Access to the royal treasury for mourning expenses.
Admission to the Privy Council.

trials.prompt: What grievance drives Hamlet's first soliloquy, 'O, that this too too solid flesh would melt'?
options: Claudius's murder of his father, which he has just discovered.
His mother's rapid remarriage to his uncle.
The threat of invasion from young Fortinbras.
Ophelia's rejection of his love-letters.

trials.prompt: When Hamlet calls Claudius 'no more like my father / Than I to Hercules,' what is he asserting?
options: That he resembles Hercules in strength.
That he, like Claudius, is unimposing — but the resemblance between the two kings is even weaker.
That Hercules is the proper Greek analog for his father.
That Claudius fancies himself a Hercules and must be disabused.

trials.prompt: What does Horatio tell Hamlet at the end of the scene?
options: That Claudius poisoned his father's wine at a banquet.
That a figure resembling the late King has appeared on the watch platform at night.
That Ophelia has refused his gifts and letters.
That Fortinbras has crossed into Danish territory.
